levels.title: Chaining Commands
levels.narrative: The server has a massive user list. You need to find which users are admins by reading the file and searching the output. Two commands, connected by a pipe.
levels.riddle:
A list of users, long and wide,
But only 'admin' ones you need to find inside.
Chain two commands with a pipe so grand,
Read the file, then search — hand in hand.

files.users.txt:
user:john:viewer
user:sarah:editor
user:phantom:admin
user:mike:viewer
user:lisa:editor
user:root:admin
user:guest:viewer
user:alex:editor
user:system:admin

hints: You need to read a file AND search its contents in one command.
The '|' (pipe) sends the output of one command as input to another.

levels.title: Counting Words
levels.narrative: The server's error log might be corrupted. You need to count how many lines it has to verify its integrity. The expected count is displayed, and you must output the actual count.
levels.riddle:
How many lines does the error log hold?
Count them up — let the number be told.
A command exists that counts with ease,
Lines, words, or bytes — take your pick, if you please.

files.error.log:
ERROR: Disk read failure at sector 42
ERROR: Memory allocation failed
ERROR: Network timeout on port 8080
ERROR: Authentication failure for user 'ghost'
ERROR: File not found: /secret/data.enc
ERROR: Permission denied: /root/vault
ERROR: Connection refused from 192.168.1.42
ERROR: Buffer overflow detected
ERROR: Segmentation fault in module X
ERROR: Stack trace dumped to core.log
files.expected.txt: The error log should have exactly 10 lines.

hints: You need to count the number of lines in a file.
The 'wc' command counts lines (-l), words (-w), or characters (-c).

levels.title: Order from Chaos
levels.narrative: The access log shows IP addresses that connected to the server. Some appear multiple times. Find the duplicate entries by sorting the list and filtering unique values.
levels.riddle:
IPs connect, some more than once,
Sort them out — don't be a dunce.
Pipe through sort, then find what's shared,
Duplicates revealed, none will be spared.
hints: You need to find which IPs appear more than once. Sort first, then find duplicates.
'sort' orders lines, 'uniq -d' shows only duplicates (requires sorted input).

levels.title: The Filtered Log
levels.narrative: The server's main log contains thousands of entries of different types: INFO, WARN, ERROR. You need to find out how many ERROR entries there are and which are most common. Chain multiple commands together!
levels.riddle:
Errors hide in a sea of log lines,
Filter, sort, and count — read between the signs.
How many of each error type appear?
Chain your commands, make the answer clear.

files.main.log:
INFO: System started
ERROR: disk_failure
WARN: Low memory
ERROR: network_timeout
INFO: User login
ERROR: disk_failure
WARN: High CPU usage
ERROR: auth_failure
INFO: Backup complete
ERROR: network_timeout
WARN: Low disk space
ERROR: disk_failure
INFO: System check OK
ERROR: network_timeout
ERROR: auth_failure
INFO: Service restarted
ERROR: disk_failure
WARN: Memory pressure
ERROR: network_timeout
ERROR: auth_failure

hints: First filter only ERROR lines, then sort them, then count unique occurrences.
Chain: grep to filter, sort to order, uniq -c to count.
Try: grep ERROR main.log | sort | uniq -c

levels.title: Redirect and Conquer
levels.narrative: The phantom_admin left a final test. Extract all lines containing 'SECRET' from multiple files and save the results to a file called 'collected_secrets.txt'. The server will verify the output file.
levels.riddle:
Secrets scattered across the land,
Gather them all with a single command.
Search through everything, collect what you find,
Redirect the output — leave nothing behind.

files.file1.txt:
Normal data here
SECRET: alpha_protocol
More normal data
files.file2.txt:
Just regular stuff
Nothing to see here
files.file3.txt:
SECRET: omega_protocol
Regular information
SECRET: delta_protocol

hints: Search for 'SECRET' across all files and save the results to a new file.
Use grep to search, then '>' to redirect output into a file.
